title: Slow Stretch: Three Movements, Three Minutes
subtitle: A three-minute embodiment practice for returning to the physical body through gentle movement
excerpt: A three-minute embodiment practice using three simple movements: a neck side-stretch, a shoulder roll, and a standing forward fold. No equipment, no flexibility required.
sourceNotes: Original to homemade.education. Draws on standard somatic awareness and movement practices.

practice.whenToUse: Use on mornings when you are stuck in your head and have lost contact with your body, or after a long stretch of sitting without moving.
practice.whenNotToUse: Skip or modify if you have a current neck, shoulder, or back injury.

content.text: Three movements, three minutes, no equipment. The goal is not flexibility or exercise. It is the return of attention to the physical body: noticing where you are tight, where you are holding, and where you have warmth.

Stand or sit comfortably. Drop your right ear slowly toward your right shoulder. Do not force it, just let the weight of the head stretch the left side of the neck. Hold for four slow breaths. Notice where you feel the stretch. Lift back to centre slowly. Repeat on the left side.

Lift both shoulders up toward your ears on an inhale. On the exhale, roll them back and down. Do this five times, moving slowly. Then reverse: lift, roll forward and down. Five times. Notice the difference between the two directions. Where does the tightness live?

Standing, with feet hip-width apart. On an exhale, let your upper body fold slowly forward. Let your head hang. Let your arms hang. Bend your knees as much as you need to. This is not a hamstring stretch. It is a release. Stay for four to six slow breaths. On an inhale, unfurl slowly back to standing, one vertebra at a time, head coming up last.
Stand still for a moment after the fold. Notice your feet on the floor. Notice your breath. That is the whole practice.
Original to homemade.education. It draws on how people use slow movement to come back to the body.
